patterns.name: Singleton
patterns.description: Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point.
patterns.core_concept: One instance only — trade testability for global access control
questions: What problem does Singleton solve, and what's its main tradeoff?
How would you implement a thread-safe Singleton in Python?
When would you prefer dependency injection over Singleton?
How does Singleton violate the Single Responsibility Principle?
What's the difference between lazy and eager initialization in Singleton?
How would you test code that depends on a Singleton? What makes it hard?
Give a real-world example where Singleton is appropriate vs. where it's an antipattern.

patterns.name: Factory Method
patterns.description: Defines an interface for creating objects, but lets subclasses decide which classes to instantiate.
patterns.core_concept: Defer object creation to subclasses — decouple client from concrete types
questions: What's the key difference between Factory Method and a simple factory function?
How does Factory Method follow the Open/Closed Principle?
Design a notification system using Factory Method for Email, SMS, and Push notifications.
When would you choose Abstract Factory over Factory Method?
How does Factory Method relate to polymorphism?
What problem does Factory Method solve that constructor calls don't?
Implement a logger factory where the concrete logger (file, console, remote) is determined at runtime.

patterns.name: Abstract Factory
patterns.description: Creates families of related objects without specifying concrete classes.
patterns.core_concept: Create families of related objects — enforce product consistency without naming concrete types
questions: What's the difference between Abstract Factory and Factory Method?
Design a UI toolkit using Abstract Factory that supports Windows and Mac themes.
How do you add a new product family to an Abstract Factory without modifying existing code?
What principle does Abstract Factory implement to enforce product consistency?
When would you choose Abstract Factory — what's the telltale sign you need it?
How does Abstract Factory handle the dependency inversion principle?
What are the downsides of Abstract Factory (maintainability, extensibility)?

patterns.name: Builder
patterns.description: Constructs complex objects step by step, separating construction from representation.
patterns.core_concept: Build complex objects step-by-step — separate construction from representation
questions: What problem does Builder solve that a constructor with many parameters doesn't?
Implement a QueryBuilder for SQL with select, where, orderBy, limit steps.
What's the Director role in the Builder pattern — when do you need it?
How does Builder compare to the telescoping constructor antipattern?
When would you use Builder vs Factory Method?
How do fluent interfaces (method chaining) relate to Builder?
Design a Builder for a Pizza object with optional toppings, size, and crust type.

patterns.name: Prototype
patterns.description: Creates new objects by cloning an existing object (the prototype).
patterns.core_concept: Clone existing objects to skip expensive initialization — watch shallow vs deep copy
questions: What's the difference between shallow copy and deep copy in the context of Prototype?
When is Prototype more efficient than using a constructor?
How would you implement Prototype for a complex object graph with circular references?
What problem does Prototype solve in systems where object initialization is expensive?
How does Python's copy.deepcopy relate to the Prototype pattern?
Design a game enemy spawning system using Prototype.
What are the pitfalls of shallow copying when using Prototype?

patterns.name: Adapter
patterns.description: Allows incompatible interfaces to work together by wrapping one interface to match another.
patterns.core_concept: Wrap an incompatible interface to match what the client expects — the 'translator' pattern
questions: What's the difference between class adapter and object adapter?
Design an Adapter to make a legacy payment system work with a new payment interface.
How does Adapter differ from Facade?
When would you use Adapter vs. rewriting the interface directly?
Implement an Adapter that makes a third-party JSON API work with your XML-based codebase.
How does Adapter follow the Open/Closed Principle?
What are the composition vs. inheritance tradeoffs in Adapter?

patterns.name: Bridge
patterns.description: Decouples abstraction from implementation so both can vary independently.
patterns.core_concept: Decouple abstraction from implementation so both can evolve independently — prefer composition
questions: What problem does Bridge solve that inheritance doesn't handle well?
Design a bridge for rendering shapes (Circle, Square) on different platforms (Windows, Mac).
How does Bridge differ from Adapter?
When does class hierarchy explosion indicate you need Bridge?
How does Bridge implement the 'prefer composition over inheritance' principle?
What's the 'abstraction' and what's the 'implementation' in Bridge?
How would you extend a Bridge-based system to add new abstractions and implementations?

patterns.name: Composite
patterns.description: Composes objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies.
patterns.core_concept: Treat individual objects and compositions uniformly — recursive tree structure
questions: What's the key interface that both leaf and composite nodes must implement?
Design a file system using Composite (files and directories).
How does Composite simplify client code when working with tree structures?
What's the tradeoff of Composite (when does it overly generalize)?
How would you implement a total size calculation for a composite file system?
How does Composite relate to recursive data structures?
When would you use Composite vs. a simple recursive function?

patterns.name: Decorator
patterns.description: Attaches additional responsibilities to objects dynamically by wrapping them.
patterns.core_concept: Add behavior by wrapping objects at runtime — avoid subclass explosion
questions: How does Decorator differ from inheritance for adding behavior?
Design a coffee ordering system using Decorator (add milk, sugar, espresso shots).
What's the key requirement for the Decorator to be transparent to clients?
How do Python's @decorators relate to the Decorator design pattern?
What's the order of decoration and why does it matter?
When would you choose Decorator over subclassing?
What are the downsides of deep decorator chains?

patterns.name: Facade
patterns.description: Provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem.
patterns.core_concept: Single simplified entry point to a complex subsystem — doesn't restrict access, just simplifies it
questions: What's the difference between Facade and Adapter?
Design a home theater Facade for a complex system of TV, sound, streaming, lights.
Does Facade restrict access to the subsystem, or just provide a simpler interface?
How does Facade help with the 'Law of Demeter'?
When would layered Facades make sense?
How does Facade relate to the 'separation of concerns' principle?
What's the risk of a Facade becoming a 'god object'?

patterns.name: Flyweight
patterns.description: Shares common state among many objects to support large numbers efficiently.
patterns.core_concept: Share intrinsic state across many objects — trade CPU for memory
questions: What's the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic state in Flyweight?
Design a text editor using Flyweight for character objects.
When does Flyweight make sense vs. just using a cache?
How does Flyweight factory manage shared instances?
What are the memory vs. CPU tradeoffs in Flyweight?
How would you implement Flyweight in a particle system for a game?
What makes Flyweight objects immutable by necessity?

patterns.name: Proxy
patterns.description: Provides a surrogate or placeholder to control access to another object.
patterns.core_concept: Control access to another object — add lazy load, caching, logging, or protection transparently
questions: What are the 4 main types of Proxy (virtual, protection, remote, logging)?
Design a caching proxy for an expensive database query object.
How does Proxy differ from Decorator?
How would you implement a lazy-loading proxy for a large image?
When would you use a protection proxy for access control?
How does a remote proxy hide network communication from clients?
What's the interface requirement for Proxy to be transparent?

patterns.name: Chain of Responsibility
patterns.description: Passes requests along a chain of handlers, each deciding to handle or pass on.
patterns.core_concept: Pass requests along a handler chain until one claims it — decouple sender from receiver
questions: How does Chain of Responsibility decouple senders from receivers?
Design a support ticket escalation system (L1→L2→L3) using CoR.
What's the difference between Chain of Responsibility and a pipeline?
How do you handle the case where no handler handles the request?
When would you choose CoR over a simple if-else chain?
How does CoR relate to middleware in web frameworks like Express.js?
What are the performance implications of long chains?

patterns.name: Command
patterns.description: Encapsulates a request as an object, enabling undo/redo, queuing, and logging.
patterns.core_concept: Encapsulate requests as objects — enable undo, queue, and audit trail
questions: What are the 4 components of Command pattern (invoker, command, receiver, client)?
Design an undo/redo system for a text editor using Command.
How does Command enable transaction-like behavior?
How does Command support queuing and scheduled execution?
When would you use Command vs. a callback function?
How does Command implement the 'encapsulate what varies' principle?
Design a macro-recording feature using Command.

patterns.name: Iterator
patterns.description: Provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its implementation.
patterns.core_concept: Traverse a collection without exposing its internal structure — uniform access interface
questions: What interface must an Iterator implement?
How does Iterator decouple traversal logic from the collection?
Design a tree iterator that traverses nodes in-order without exposing tree internals.
How do Python's __iter__ and __next__ implement the Iterator pattern?
What's the difference between internal and external iterators?
When would you implement a custom iterator vs. use a generator?
How do lazy iterators save memory vs. materializing a full list?

patterns.name: Mediator
patterns.description: Reduces direct dependencies between components by centralizing communication.
patterns.core_concept: Replace many-to-many component connections with one-to-many through a central hub
questions: How does Mediator reduce the number of connections between components?
Design a chat room using Mediator where users communicate through a central server.
What's the risk of Mediator becoming a 'god object'?
How does Mediator differ from Observer?
When should you use Mediator vs. direct component communication?
How does the air traffic control system exemplify Mediator?
How does Mediator relate to the 'star topology' vs. 'mesh topology' tradeoff?

patterns.name: Memento
patterns.description: Captures and externalizes an object's internal state so it can be restored later.
patterns.core_concept: Capture and restore object state without breaking encapsulation — the snapshot pattern
questions: What are the 3 participants of Memento (originator, memento, caretaker)?
How does Memento preserve encapsulation while saving state?
Design an undo/redo system for a drawing app using Memento.
How does Memento differ from Command pattern for implementing undo?
What are the memory implications of storing many mementos?
How would you implement incremental mementos instead of full snapshots?
When is Memento better than serialization for state capture?

patterns.name: Observer
patterns.description: Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, dependents are notified.
patterns.core_concept: One publisher, many subscribers — notify dependents automatically on state change
questions: What's the difference between push and pull notification in Observer?
Design an event system for a stock ticker using Observer.
What's the 'dangling observer' problem and how do you avoid it?
How does Observer relate to the pub/sub pattern?
How does Observer implement the dependency inversion principle?
When would you use Observer vs. direct method calls?
How do you handle ordering guarantees with multiple observers?

patterns.name: State
patterns.description: Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes.
patterns.core_concept: Replace conditionals with state objects — behavior changes as state changes
questions: How does State eliminate complex if/else or switch statements?
Design a traffic light system using State pattern.
What's the difference between State and Strategy pattern?
When does a state machine become complex enough to warrant the State pattern?
How do state objects transition to other states?
Can a State have multiple contexts? What are the implications?
Design a vending machine with State (idle, has_money, dispensing, out_of_stock).

patterns.name: Strategy
patterns.description: Defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable.
patterns.core_concept: Swap algorithms at runtime — encapsulate what varies, favor composition over inheritance
questions: How does Strategy implement the Open/Closed Principle?
Design a sorting strategy system where the algorithm is selected at runtime.
What's the difference between Strategy and State?
How does Strategy eliminate conditional logic in the context class?
When would you use Strategy vs. a simple function parameter?
Design a payment processing system with Strategy (credit card, PayPal, crypto).
How do Python's key= parameters in sort() relate to Strategy?

patterns.name: Template Method
patterns.description: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, deferring some steps to subclasses.
patterns.core_concept: Define algorithm skeleton in base class, defer steps to subclasses — Hollywood Principle
questions: What's the difference between Template Method and Strategy?
Design a data mining framework using Template Method for CSV, PDF, and DOC files.
What are 'hooks' in Template Method and when do you use them?
How does Template Method implement the Hollywood Principle ('don't call us, we'll call you')?
When would you prefer Strategy (composition) over Template Method (inheritance)?
How does Template Method encourage code reuse across subclasses?
What are the risks of deep Template Method hierarchies?

patterns.name: Visitor
patterns.description: Lets you add new operations to objects without modifying their classes.
patterns.core_concept: Add new operations to objects without modifying them — double dispatch separates algorithm from structure
questions: What's 'double dispatch' and how does Visitor use it?
Design an AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) with Visitor for compilation passes.
How does Visitor violate the Open/Closed Principle for adding new element types?
When would you choose Visitor vs. adding methods directly to the element classes?
How does Visitor separate algorithms from the object structure?
What's the performance cost of Visitor's double dispatch?
Design a shape area calculator using Visitor for Circle, Rectangle, Triangle.

patterns.name: Interpreter
patterns.description: Defines a grammar for a language and provides an interpreter to deal with that grammar.
patterns.core_concept: Represent grammar rules as objects — parse and evaluate expressions or DSLs
questions: When would you implement a full Interpreter vs. use an existing parser?
Design a simple expression interpreter for arithmetic (1+2*3).
How does Composite pattern relate to Interpreter?
What are the performance limitations of the Interpreter pattern for complex grammars?
How would you extend an Interpreter to support new expressions without modifying existing ones?
What's the difference between Interpreter and Template Method for handling grammar?
Give a real example where Interpreter is used (regex, SQL, config DSLs).

patterns.name: Single Responsibility Principle
patterns.description: A class should have only one reason to change — one job, one owner.
patterns.core_concept: One class, one job — if two things change for different reasons, they belong in different classes
questions: How do you define 'responsibility' in SRP — what's the test for a single responsibility?
Refactor a User class that handles authentication, profile updates, and email sending to follow SRP.
How does SRP relate to cohesion and coupling?
Is SRP only about classes, or does it apply to functions and modules too?
When does following SRP too strictly lead to over-engineering?
How do you identify SRP violations in a code review?
How does SRP make testing easier? Give a concrete example.

patterns.name: Open/Closed Principle
patterns.description: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification.
patterns.core_concept: Add behavior by extending, not editing — protect existing code from change-induced bugs
questions: What does 'open for extension, closed for modification' mean in practice?
How do Strategy and Template Method patterns directly implement OCP?
Design a discount system that can add new discount types without modifying existing code.
When is it acceptable to violate OCP — is it always wrong to modify existing code?
How does OCP relate to the need for abstraction and interfaces?
How does OCP apply to plugin architectures and feature flags?
What's the relationship between OCP and the 'shotgun surgery' code smell?

patterns.name: Liskov Substitution Principle
patterns.description: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering program correctness.
patterns.core_concept: A subclass must honor the contract of its parent — behavioral compatibility, not just type compatibility
questions: Why does the classic Square extends Rectangle example violate LSP?
What are the three contract rules LSP requires (preconditions, postconditions, invariants)?
How does violating LSP break the Open/Closed Principle at the call site?
Design a Bird hierarchy that avoids LSP violations with non-flying birds.
How does LSP relate to interface design — what makes a good base type?
What's the 'is-a' vs 'behaves-like-a' distinction and why does it matter for inheritance?
How would you detect LSP violations in a code review?

patterns.name: Interface Segregation Principle
patterns.description: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use.
patterns.core_concept: Split fat interfaces into focused ones — don't force clients to implement unused methods
questions: What's the sign that an interface violates ISP?
Refactor a fat IAnimal interface (eat, sleep, fly, swim) to follow ISP.
How does ISP relate to the Single Responsibility Principle?
How does ISP affect class coupling and testability?
When does splitting interfaces go too far — what's the granularity sweet spot?
How does ISP apply to REST API design (not just OOP)?
How do role interfaces vs. header interfaces relate to ISP?

patterns.name: Dependency Inversion Principle
patterns.description: High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions.
patterns.core_concept: Depend on abstractions not concretions — invert the ownership of dependencies with interfaces
questions: What's the difference between Dependency Inversion Principle and Dependency Injection?
How does DIP eliminate tight coupling between high-level and low-level modules?
Refactor a ReportGenerator that directly instantiates a MySQLDatabase to follow DIP.
How does DIP enable swapping implementations without changing the high-level module?
How do DI containers (Spring, NestJS) implement DIP automatically?
What's the relationship between DIP and the Abstract Factory pattern?
How does DIP make unit testing easier? Show a concrete before/after example.
